How the invisible webs of nature, community, and technology determine whether we survive or collapse.
What Connects a Starving Settler to an Office Worker?
If you look at the world around us, it looks like a collection of separate things. You see a tree, a person, a computer, or an old ruin made of stone. But if you look closer, the way a scientist looks through a microscope, you start to see that nothing actually stands alone. Everything is tied together by invisible threads.
Think of a massive tree with roots spreading deep underground and branches reaching high into the sky. The roots hold the soil, the soil keeps the water clean, the water feeds the tree, and the tree provides oxygen for us to breathe. If you cut the roots, the whole thing falls down. The same rule applies to human lives. No one survives by themselves. Our health, our families, our jobs, and the cities we live in all depend on networks of connection. When these networks are strong, we thrive; when they break, everything falls apart.
There is a way to look at this scientifically, and we call it Relatology-the study of relationship fields. A person’s life is not just made of their own choices. It is shaped by a whole field of connections called a relatement. This includes your family, the laws of your town, the ecosystems that give you water, and even the digital tools on your phone. When we ignore these connections, we run into what experts call a polycrisis-a big word for a simple problem where multiple crises like climate change, loneliness, and breaking systems all crash into each other at the same time. To fix this, we have to understand the rules of the web.
How Does a Mountain Guard a People?
For thousands of years, people lived without modern jail houses, lawyers, or written legal codes, yet their communities did not fall into chaos. How did they do it? They used trust and shared values instead of metal locks and keys. Indigenous cultures have passed down traditions for centuries that are not just beautiful stories told around a fire; they are highly practical instructions for staying alive.
Take the way the Potawatomi people gather sweetgrass in the forest. The rule is incredibly simple: take only what you need, take only what is given, never take more than half, and make sure you use everything you take. A child doesn’t learn this in a standard classroom with a blackboard. A child learns it by walking through the woods with a parent, picking berries or harvesting roots. It is an active lesson in reciprocity-the idea that if you take care of the land, the land will take care of you.
An Aboriginal elder named Guboo Ted Thomas explained it clearly when he said that the elders guard the Law, and the Law guards the people. This law does not come from a government building; it comes from the mountain and the land itself. When you look at the world through this lens, nature is not a collection of raw materials to be mined and sold for profit. Nature is a partner. If we treat the Earth like a machine to be driven until it breaks, we lose the very systems that give us food, clean water, and a stable climate.
Why Do Some Neighbors Forget to Die?
We often think that being healthy is just a matter of personal willpower. We tell ourselves that if we eat our vegetables, run on a treadmill, and avoid bad habits, we will live a long time. But medical science shows us that something else matters just as much as what we put into our bodies: the people we surround ourselves with.
In places called Blue Zones-like Okinawa in Japan, Sardinia in Italy, and Nicoya in Costa Rica-people frequently live past the age of 100. They do not possess secret vitamins or magic fountains. Instead, they possess incredibly strong social webs. In Okinawa, people form groups called moai, which are small teams of friends who stick together for their entire lives, sharing money, chores, and emotional support. In Sardinia, older men gather in the village square every single day to talk and laugh.
Look at what happens when these ties are missing. Scientific studies show that being lonely or isolated raises your risk of heart disease by 29 percent and stroke by 32 percent. That is just as dangerous as smoking cigarettes or being severely overweight. When you have close friends and family, your body actually produces less stress hormones like cortisol, which keeps your immune system strong. Health is not just an individual property inside your skin; it is an emergent property of your relationships.
Can a Dashboard Build Real Trust?
Today, a new kind of disruptor is stepping into our relational fields: artificial intelligence. Consider the story of an office worker whose job gets replaced by an AI system because software can do the work faster, cheaper, and without taking a vacation. From a pure accounting perspective, the company wins because it slashes payroll costs. But if you look deeper through the lens of Relatology, you see that automation can hollow out the invisible trust that keeps a business alive.
We saw this happen in the real world with a financial technology company named Klarna. They laid off 700 human workers and shifted customer service tasks to AI tools. Shortly after, their customer service quality plummeted, and the CEO had to admit they went too far, forcing the company to scramble and rehire human staff.
When corporations use technology simply to cut costs, they often push the risks downward onto individual workers while moving the profits upward. It is similar to the rideshare model where drivers are called independent contractors but have to pay for their own insurance, fuel, and car repairs on their own dime. A relationship where one side takes all the risk while the other side takes all the reward is brittle and unstable. Technology works best when it acts as a tool to support human care, speed, and clarity, rather than trying to automate the human soul out of our work.
What Happens When We Strain the Earth Past Its Limit?
History shows us that when human societies consume far more than their environment can replenish, they enter a state called overshoot. Overshoot acts like a slow poison in the relational field. It does not just mean running out of trees or water; it strains the human relationships that hold civilization together until they snap.
Look at the ancient Norse settlers in Greenland during the 1400s. They built farms and churches just like the ones they remembered in Europe. But as the climate turned colder, they refused to change their ways. They kept raising cattle and cutting down sparse timber even as the grasslands shrank away. Most importantly, they ignored the smart hunting and fishing practices of their neighbors, the Inuit, who knew how to live in harmony with the ice. Because of this worldview rigidity, their colony completely disappeared.
A similar pattern emerged in Chaco Canyon in the American Southwest. The Ancestral Puebloans constructed massive stone houses and complex road systems. But over time, they cut down too many forests and exhausted their soil. When a severe drought struck in the late 1100s, the damaged land could no longer support them, and the entire civilization collapsed because its ecological foundation had been hollowed out from within. Today, we face a global version of this same problem, marked by Earth Overshoot Day moving all the way from late December in 1970 to early August today.
How Do We Build a Web That Holds?
Collapse is not an unavoidable destiny, but avoiding it requires conscious care for the ties that keep us alive. We can see this by looking at historical survivors who faced massive pressures but chose to adapt instead of breaking.
During the Tokugawa era in Japan, between the 1600s and 1800s, the island faced severe deforestation and soil erosion. Instead of collapsing, the leaders completely reorganized society around strict forest management, advanced crop rotation, and a culture that rejected wasteful luxury. They treated their forests as long-term partners, stabilizing their society for centuries. We see the same success story on the island of Hispaniola. Haiti cut down almost all its forests, leading to soil erosion and deep poverty. Meanwhile, the Dominican Republic invested heavily in protecting its trees and watersheds, creating a stable, resilient foundation for its people.
(Source: haitifriends.org; National Geographic)
Resilience begins with the small choices we make every single day. It means checking in on a lonely neighbor, sharing the work of caring for children or elders, and supporting local farms that respect the limits of the soil. We must drop the old, out-of-date story that says endless economic growth is the only goal, and that the Earth is just property to be owned and exploited. The future belongs to those who realize that survival is not a solo game. We survive only when we learn to live in harmonious, cooperative relationships with each other and with the living planet.
Key Takeaways

• Survival Is Relational: No individual, company, or country survives completely alone; our safety and well-being depend entirely on the strength of our hidden connections.

• Overshoot Causes Collapse: Consuming resources faster than nature can grow them strains human systems, breaks trust, and drives societies toward systemic failure.

• Loneliness Destroys Health: Strong relationships protect our hearts and minds more than medicine alone, while isolation carries the same health risks as smoking.

• Technology Requires Balance: Implementing tools like AI can cut short-term payroll costs, but if it destroys trust with customers and workers, the system becomes weak and brittle.

• Worldview Adaptability is Safety: Societies that endure are the ones willing to change their core habits and stories to match the true limits of their environment.
Inspiration
Inspired by the Living in Relationship series and the book Relatology by the original author.
David Spearman
